questions.prompt: No searching: what is a primary role of an operating-system kernel?

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Manage hardware resources and provide protected services to programs.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Manage hardware resources and provide protected services to programs.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Manage hardware resources and provide protected services to programs.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Compilation is performed by development tools, not the kernel's core runtime role.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Compilation is performed by development tools, not the kernel's core runtime role.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Compilation is performed by development tools, not the kernel's core runtime role.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: System calls provide controlled entry to kernel services.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. System calls provide controlled entry to kernel services.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. System calls provide controlled entry to kernel services.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: what does virtual memory give a process?

answers.text: The practical answer is: direct unrestricted access to all physical memory.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Address translation and protection isolate process memory.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Address translation and protection isolate process memory.

answers.text: My answer: Different processes can use the same virtual address for different physical storage.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Different processes can use the same virtual address for different physical storage.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Different processes can use the same virtual address for different physical storage.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Its own virtual address space mapped to physical memory and other backing.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Its own virtual address space mapped to physical memory and other backing.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Its own virtual address space mapped to physical memory and other backing.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: what happens in a context switch?

answers.text: I'd go with this: A context switch can occur only between different processes, never between threads.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The scheduler can switch between threads, including threads in the same process.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The scheduler can switch between threads, including threads in the same process.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: the system saves one execution context and restores another.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The system saves one execution context and restores another.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The system saves one execution context and restores another.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, the CPU permanently erases the previous process.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The previous task can resume from its saved state.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The previous task can resume from its saved state.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: does every page fault indicate a program bug?

answers.text: I'd go with this: Many page faults are handled normally by loading or mapping a page.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Many page faults are handled normally by loading or mapping a page.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Many page faults are handled normally by loading or mapping a page.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: an invalid access can produce a fault the OS cannot resolve for the process.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. An invalid access can produce a fault the OS cannot resolve for the process.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. An invalid access can produce a fault the OS cannot resolve for the process.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, demand paging relies on recoverable page faults.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Demand paging relies on recoverable page faults.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Demand paging relies on recoverable page faults.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: how do threads within one process normally relate?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, each thread has a completely separate copy of all process memory.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Threads share memory but have separate stacks and execution state.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Threads share memory but have separate stacks and execution state.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Shared memory makes communication cheap but introduces synchronization risks.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Shared memory makes communication cheap but introduces synchronization risks.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Shared memory makes communication cheap but introduces synchronization risks.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: They share the process address space and resources.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. They share the process address space and resources.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. They share the process address space and resources.

questions.prompt: Forum check: how does a counting semaphore differ from a mutex?

answers.text: From what I've seen, declaring a semaphore automatically protects shared data even when code ignores its protocol.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Synchronization works only when all relevant accesses follow the semaphore protocol correctly.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Synchronization works only when all relevant accesses follow the semaphore protocol correctly.

answers.text: The practical answer is: a semaphore can represent multiple available permits.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A semaphore can represent multiple available permits.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A semaphore can represent multiple available permits.

answers.text: My answer: A semaphore always has one owner that must release it.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Ownership is a mutex concept; semaphore use need not enforce the same owner.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Ownership is a mutex concept; semaphore use need not enforce the same owner.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: what makes a race condition possible?

answers.text: Short version: The result depends on uncontrolled timing of concurrent accesses.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The result depends on uncontrolled timing of concurrent accesses.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The result depends on uncontrolled timing of concurrent accesses.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Merely having two functions in the same source file.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Concurrency and shared or interacting state are required for a timing race.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Concurrency and shared or interacting state are required for a timing race.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: synchronization can establish a defined order or atomic access.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Synchronization can establish a defined order or atomic access.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Synchronization can establish a defined order or atomic access.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: which conditions are associated with classic resource deadlock?

answers.text: The practical answer is: high CPU utilization alone.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Load can be high without any circular resource dependency.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Load can be high without any circular resource dependency.

answers.text: My answer: Breaking one necessary condition can prevent this form of deadlock.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Breaking one necessary condition can prevent this form of deadlock.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Breaking one necessary condition can prevent this form of deadlock.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: what tradeoff does a CPU scheduler manage?

answers.text: Short version: Giving a task lower priority guarantees that it will still run before starvation is possible.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Priority scheduling needs an explicit fairness or aging policy to prevent starvation.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Priority scheduling needs an explicit fairness or aging policy to prevent starvation.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Responsiveness, throughput, fairness, and overhead.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Responsiveness, throughput, fairness, and overhead.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Responsiveness, throughput, fairness, and overhead.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: it can maximize every metric simultaneously for every workload.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Scheduling goals conflict and depend on workload.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Scheduling goals conflict and depend on workload.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: what is copy-on-write after process creation?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Processes initially share pages until one attempts to modify a shared page.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Processes initially share pages until one attempts to modify a shared page.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Processes initially share pages until one attempts to modify a shared page.

answers.text: The way I understand it: every page is copied before the child can run.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Copy-on-write postpones copies and avoids copying untouched pages.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Copy-on-write postpones copies and avoids copying untouched pages.

answers.text: Short version: A write fault can trigger creation of a private copy.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A write fault can trigger creation of a private copy.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A write fault can trigger creation of a private copy.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: what is virtual-memory thrashing?

answers.text: I'd go with this: A CPU executes instructions too quickly for memory.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Thrashing comes from an insufficient working set and heavy paging.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Thrashing comes from an insufficient working set and heavy paging.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: reducing active working sets or adding memory can help.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Reducing active working sets or adding memory can help.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Reducing active working sets or adding memory can help.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, the system spends excessive time moving pages instead of doing useful work.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The system spends excessive time moving pages instead of doing useful work.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The system spends excessive time moving pages instead of doing useful work.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: why does an operating system cache file data in memory?

answers.text: The way I understand it: the operating-system page cache is the same hardware structure as a CPU cache.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The page cache is managed by the kernel for file data; CPU caches are hardware caches for memory accesses.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The page cache is managed by the kernel for file data; CPU caches are hardware caches for memory accesses.

answers.text: Short version: Reading file data from the page cache always performs a new disk read.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A cache hit can satisfy the read from memory without accessing the storage device again.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A cache hit can satisfy the read from memory without accessing the storage device again.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Cached writes are guaranteed durable immediately.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Durability requires the data to reach the required stable storage boundary.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Durability requires the data to reach the required stable storage boundary.
